items.feature: Legacy contest enumeration
items.behavior: The contest catalog exposes every TSimContest member in its declared order.

items.feature: Legacy run mode enumeration
items.behavior: Session lifecycle behavior accounts for every legacy TRunMode value.

items.feature: Legacy contest definitions
items.behavior: The contest catalog preserves the identity, ordering, exchange fields, editability, and defaults of every legacy contest definition.

items.feature: Legacy persisted settings
items.behavior: Settings import and persistence account for every INI key read, written, migrated, or deleted by the legacy implementation.

items.feature: Legacy main-form objects
items.behavior: The product retains the information, editable fields, controls, indicators, and presentation roles exposed by every legacy main-form object.

items.feature: Legacy main-menu commands
items.behavior: Every legacy menu command remains available through an equivalent keyboard-first or pointer-accessible workflow.

items.feature: Legacy main-form event bindings and handlers
items.behavior: Every legacy main-form event binding and referenced handler is represented by an equivalent semantic engine command or transient UX interaction.

items.feature: Legacy shortcuts and keyboard branches
items.behavior: Every declared shortcut and direct keyboard branch remains available with equivalent focus-sensitive behavior.

items.feature: Legacy QSO record and error model
items.behavior: The QSO log retains every recorded value, verified value, error classification, correction flag, multiplier, point, and duplicate indicator used by legacy results.

items.feature: Legacy logging, scoring, rate, correction, and result paths
items.behavior: Every legacy log operation and score-table definition remains represented in deterministic logging, correction, score, rate, and result behavior.

items.feature: Legacy simulation state models and transitions
items.behavior: Every contest, station, and simulated-operator state and observed state transition is represented in the deterministic session state machine.

items.feature: Legacy contest, station, and operator routines
items.behavior: Every production routine participating in contest advancement, message exchange, station behavior, operator decisions, and station collection management is represented by acceptance coverage.

items.feature: Legacy portable keying and DSP processing
items.behavior: Every legacy Morse envelope, mixer, filter, modulation, AGC, sound-data type, constant, property, and processing routine is represented by deterministic parity coverage.

items.feature: Legacy sound output, buffering, and WAV adapters
items.behavior: Legacy device selection, bounded buffering, playback lifecycle, WAV read/write, and adapter failure behavior remain available behind portable engine-owned audio contracts.

items.feature: Legacy VCL-only hint and volume controls
items.behavior: The permanent hint and volume slider interaction roles remain represented in the cross-platform UX without retaining VCL dependencies.

items.feature: Legacy contest-specific implementations
items.behavior: Every contest-specific type, property, and production routine remains represented in deterministic rule, exchange, multiplier, point, and validation behavior.

items.feature: Legacy call, prefix, exchange, and serial data parsers
items.behavior: Call-list lookup, DXCC lookup, exchange fields, serial generation, callsign utilities, lexical analysis, Sweepstakes exchange parsing, contest call-history loading, and every explicit parser path preserve their legacy data model and failure behavior.

items.feature: Legacy QSB and random effects
items.behavior: QSB envelopes and seeded random distributions remain represented by deterministic simulation and DSP acceptance vectors.

items.feature: Legacy score dialog
items.behavior: Score presentation and close behavior remain available in the cross-platform UX.

items.feature: Legacy data files, recording, export, and failure paths
items.behavior: Every bundled application or data asset, distribution-file contract, command-line and external integration, generated data file, recording and export path, and explicit file, persistence, dialog, and exception operation remains available with equivalent success and failure behavior.
legacySources: bundled data and application resource files

items.feature: Legacy Lazarus and Delphi form definitions
items.behavior: Every legacy form object, event binding, keyboard shortcut, resource reference, and published form member remains represented by an equivalent keyboard-first cross-platform workflow and observable UI outcome.

items.feature: Legacy project, package, and deployment metadata
items.behavior: Every Delphi and Lazarus application, package, test-project, deployment, resource, configuration, unit-membership, and lifecycle declaration needed to build and distribute the legacy product has an explicit cross-platform counterpart or verified replacement.

items.feature: Legacy unit and smoke test contracts
items.behavior: Every active, disabled, and commented legacy test declaration, registration, fixture, lifecycle hook, method, case, and Lazarus smoke check is reviewed and represented by executable cross-implementation coverage or an equivalent port acceptance case.

items.feature: Legacy Pascal unit lifecycle hooks
items.behavior: Every legacy initialization and finalization hook executes at an equivalent lifecycle boundary with deterministic ordering, state effects, registration behavior, cleanup, and failure handling.

behavioralObligations.behavior: Every CE contest identifier is present in declaration order and selects the same contest family.
Every contest exposes CE-equivalent names, exchange types, defaults, and feature flags.
Every CE run mode is present and retains its observable selection semantics.
Certified parity always executes the pinned CE oracle and never silently substitutes a committed fixture.
The dedicated XPlat parity target executes every applicable acceptance case exactly once.
CI checks out the pinned clean CE revision and builds the hash-verified oracle before certification.
Every case retains complete replay inputs, red evidence, and a reachable first-green commit.
Manifest status, inputs, outputs, evidence classification, hashes, and Git provenance are internally consistent.
The inventory covers CE forms, projects, resources, generated files, tests, commands, data, and failure paths.
The CE oracle selects each ActiveContest before observing contest-dependent metadata or behavior.
UX, settings, data, adapters, and runtime features are certified by executed behavior rather than source tokens or filenames.
Local sidetone is mixed at the CE pipeline stage and level before filtering, modulation, and AGC.
Non-Farnsworth Morse symbols, cut numbers, unit timing, five millisecond ramps, and mark-space transitions match CE exactly.
Every CE moving-average receiver stage, reset, cascade order, and filter-swap numeric vector matches exactly.
Carrier frequency quantization, phase advance, quadrature modulation signs, and modulator numeric vectors match CE.
AGC attack, release, gain equations, state transitions, reset behavior, and output numeric vectors match CE.
Complex remote-station BFO mixing uses CE-equivalent signs, channel order, phase application, accumulation order, and output normalization.
The default CE compatibility output contract is 11025 Hz mono, 512 samples per block, and identical sample totals.
The physical audio queue preserves CE-equivalent default depth, FIFO block order, startup fill order, and exact sample continuity.
MT19937 output and every CE random distribution primitive produce deterministic numeric vectors for reviewed seeds.
QSK applies CE-equivalent receiver ducking and recovery around local transmissions.
RIT changes each station's audible offset using CE range, steps, and block-boundary timing.
Runtime bandwidth changes replace or update receiver filters at the CE-equivalent block boundary.
QRM uses CE-equivalent randomized interfering CW stations, messages, levels, pitches, speeds, retries, and lifetimes.
QRN produces CE sparse impulses and burst stations before receiver filtering and AGC.
Every remote station has an independent CE-distributed QSB process without fading the receiver noise floor.
Flutter is the CE probabilistic fast per-station QSB mode rather than a global multiplier.
Remote station amplitude and pitch use CE distributions and deterministic draw ordering.
Station BFO phase advances and resets at the same CE transmission boundaries.
SST uses independent character and spacing speeds with CE Farnsworth timing.
One seeded session random stream reproduces CE ownership and cross-feature draw order.
CE block sizes from 128 through 2048 are supported or proven import-equivalent with matching timing.
Startup warmup requests, block numbering, prefill, and alternating-filter swap timing match CE.
Base complex hiss and receiver noise floor match CE level, spectrum, random draws, and processing order.
WAV headers, PCM scaling, rounding, clipping, negative full scale, and sample bytes match CE vectors.
Recording failure and queue backpressure stop recording without faulting or allocating in the contest render path.
Raw capture, WAV, null, and physical playback receive identical ordered pre-conversion audio blocks.
Physical callbacks, underruns, drops, device loss, recovery, stale callbacks, shutdown, and disposal are bounded and proven natively.
All-effects rendering retains allocation, latency, queue-depth, underrun, drop, and GC evidence representative of sustained play.
Operator transmission completion and remote interpretation occur only after the rendered CW envelope completes.
Caller creation follows completed CQ, TU, and MyCall events with CE Poisson and no-stop rules.
Start is silent and an empty Enter initiates the appropriate CE contest CQ.
CQ, TU, operator call insertion, and periodic station-ID messages match each CE contest.
Remote exchanges retain and format every CE contest identity field, cut number, repeat, and correction variant.
CWT, SST, Field Day, NAQP, Sweepstakes, WPX, HST, and all other contests include every required CE exchange field.
ALLJA and ACAG station truth use the correct CE data columns without serial fallback.
Sweepstakes models serial, weighted precedence, own call, check, section, copied values, truth, and errors.
ARRL DX and NAQP apply CE home-call filtering and dynamic location or exchange derivation.
WPX and HST station serials use CE elapsed-time, skill, and random behavior.
HST and WPX start constraints, forced settings, activity, bandwidth, duration, and valid serial rules match CE.
Single-call delay, caller lifetime, patience, retry, Gaussian WPM, calls-from-keyer, station-ID rate, and HST call-pool removal match CE.
Mid-message append, unsent callsign correction, and abort completion preserve CE timing and station notification.
Confidence, LID, Sweepstakes correction, call-and-number repeat, and F12 probability branches match CE.
Reset and restart clear and recreate the same authoritative state at the same ordered boundaries as CE.
All 12 contest constructors expose the same exchange enum pair, default own exchange acceptance, and Farnsworth flag as CE.
Logging without a completed matching live station produces CE-compatible NIL truth rather than fabricated truth.
A call enters worked-call state only when CE would accept verified truth, so an initial error does not poison correction.
Duplicate classification requires the same prior verified-correct QSO conditions as CE.
QSO records preserve true WPM, copied and true fields, per-field errors, corrections, and contest column flags.
Wrong, incomplete, NIL, B4, and corrected training QSOs are committed with CE message and logging order.
Raw and verified points, multipliers, score, rate, and correction totals match CE after every QSO.
Automatic completion, WPX history, HST append, historical score browsing, and submission workflows match CE.
Cabrillo export includes complete interoperable QSO fields for every supported contest.
Operators can validate, persist, reload, and use a distinct own exchange for every CE contest.
F2 and ESM transmit the configured operator exchange rather than received-entry values.
Entry labels, visibility, widths, limits, filters, and focus order adapt to each contest as CE does.
Contest-specific input transforms preserve valid text and apply equivalently to keys, paste, and IME input.
Wipe clears local fields and authoritative engine ESM or active-QSO state at one semantic boundary.
Escape or Abort resets active send and ESM state and notifies stations with CE completion semantics.
Punctuation and modified Enter preserve CE TU, log-only, wrong-QSO, and field-clearing workflows.
F9 and Run Pile-Up explicitly start Pile-Up regardless of the selected run-mode control.
Every live QSK, effect, activity, speed, pitch, bandwidth, and monitor change reaches the engine through the client.
RIT, WPM, and bandwidth ranges, increments, clamping, and displayed engine state match CE.
Visible shortcut labels and commands consistently distinguish F11 Wipe from Ctrl+F11 Score.
Physical plus, equals, keypad Add, and international layouts invoke only the CE-equivalent completion actions.
All CE key-down, key-up, shortcut, mouse-wheel, tuning-reset, and monitor-reset workflows are available.
Log rows expose copied and true values, corrections, NIL, B4, duplicate, error type, and chronological status accessibly.
Start, stop, and reset clear fields, restore focus, and transition pointer affordances exactly as CE.
Tab and Shift+Tab remain in the CE high-frequency entry workflow for every contest and state.
Numeric child controls, validation, status, and failure feedback expose names, values, actions, focus, and live announcements.
The native GUI remains usable at minimum size, 100, 150, and 200 percent scaling, high contrast, and platform fonts.
Score dialogs, automatic completion, history navigation, actions, focus, and accessible content match CE.
Every legacy hint, volume, and UI component behavior remains functionally available through the cross-platform UX.
Production startup invokes CE settings migration when appropriate.
Migration translates CE indexes, ordinals, zero-or-one booleans, IDs, Hz values, duration, block size, and contest exchanges.
Migration and save preserve unknown or unconsumed CE values and are idempotent.
Clean-profile call, WPM, activity, duration, pitch, bandwidth, and related defaults match CE.
Every CE duration from 1 through 240 minutes is accepted and persists without hidden clamping.
Users can replace CE reference data through an explicit root with packaged fallback and equivalent discovery.
Every parser rejects malformed rows and reports missing or invalid replacement data without fabricating values.
Call, prefix, exchange, serial, section, lexer, and contest data parsers produce CE-equivalent values and failures.
A CE-compatible fixed WAV naming, overwrite, and discovery workflow remains available.
About, engine, assembly, CLI, archive, icon, package, and evidence version provenance agree.
Release evidence launches and discovers a real native Avalonia main window rather than setup-only execution.
Release publication requires physical-audio startup, sustained playback, effects, device change, recovery, and shutdown evidence.
In-process and remote clients cannot mutate authoritative completed QSO or result state.
The shared application service enforces the documented single active interactive session contract.
Commands apply at every documented block boundary, including during long simulation advances.
Mutating request IDs replay safely and command idempotency retention is bounded.
Lossless events and coalescible snapshots use independent bounded delivery so slow observers cannot delay or silently lose authority.
Snapshots carry a sequence watermark and reconnect never delivers older events after newer state.
Fault, operator-message, score, rate, recording, result, and control-lease events expose every authoritative transition.
Lease acquire, renew, release, takeover, identity, ordering, and transport-neutral control behavior are complete.
Identical full-session vectors produce identical commands, events, snapshots, results, and failures through in-process and gRPC clients.
Native GUI, focus, routed-key, pointer, scaling, accessibility, and shutdown evidence exists on every supported platform.
TUI, CLI, in-process, hosted gRPC, WAV, data override, migration, and archive behavior are captured on clean systems.
Every supported platform must be complete before release promotion or artifact publication.
Final certification executes every obligation live with zero skipped, waived, quarantined, expected-failure, or fixture-only cases.
Structured CE and XPlat A/B sessions cover contests, audio conditions, corrections, logging, history, and migration with experienced users.
Enter with an empty, partial, corrected, or complete call selects and sends the same ESM message and state transition as CE.
Selecting a historical QSO updates the callsign-information region to the selected call as CE does.
The UX sends semantic duration and never owns sample-rate or simulation-block conversion policy.
Help, About, readme, website, and community actions match CE availability, targets, errors, keyboard access, and focus return.
Score browsing, submission, disabled or unavailable service behavior, failures, focus, and history match CE.
Session lifecycle transitions reject invalid starts such as rmStop, define close-while-running behavior, and emit the same ordered terminal state through every client.
Every supported CE key is consumed with equivalent semantics or preserved losslessly across import, save, and restart.
CLI help, version, validation failures, runtime failures, and successful commands expose stable documented output and exit codes in packaged builds.
Legacy initialization and finalization hooks execute with equivalent order, registration, cleanup, state, and failures.

cases.behavior: For all 12 legacy contest IDs in declared order, the selected contest implementation exposes the same sent and received exchange-field enum pair, accepts the same catalog default own exchange, and enables Farnsworth timing for the same contests.
preconditions: The pinned CE oracle and toolchain fingerprint are available.
The schema-v2 fixture was captured from the pinned clean CE reference.
The 12 contest IDs are evaluated in their legacy declaration order.

assertions.firstDivergence: The zero-based first ordinal mismatch between fixture and observed string arrays, with null expected or actual only when one array ends first.
